cards.front: Sociological paradigm viewing society as interdependent parts working together for stability
Functionalist term for a recognized, intended consequence of a social structure
Functionalist term for an unintended, often unrecognized consequence of a social structure
Functionalist term for a consequence that disrupts a society's stability or equilibrium
Theorist most associated with founding structural functionalism and the concept of social solidarity
Paradigm viewing society as competition among groups over scarce resources and power
Theorist whose focus on class struggle between bourgeoisie and proletariat grounds conflict theory
Marx's term for the owners of the means of production
Marx's term for the working class who sell their labor
Micro-level paradigm focused on the meanings people attach to symbols in interaction
Theorist central to symbolic interactionism who developed the concept of the 'self'
View that social phenomena and categories are created and maintained through shared human agreement
Theory that individuals weigh perceived rewards against costs to guide social behavior
Conflict-based paradigm arguing that male-dominated society systematically oppresses women
General term for study of small-scale, face-to-face social interactions
General term for study of large-scale social structures and whole societies
Shared beliefs, values, norms, and material objects that characterize a group
Physical objects and artifacts produced by a culture
Nonmaterial elements of a culture such as beliefs, values, and language
Culture's standards for what is good, desirable, and proper
Culture's rules and expectations for behavior
Informal norms of everyday casual interaction, violation of which brings little sanction
Norms with strong moral significance, violation of which brings serious social disapproval
Norm so strongly prohibited that its violation is considered deeply forbidden or unthinkable
Norms formally written into law
Culture within a larger culture that shares distinct values while participating in the whole
Subculture whose values and norms oppose those of the dominant culture
Tendency to judge another culture by the standards of one's own, believing one's own superior
Practice of understanding a culture on its own terms without judging by outside standards
Fear or dislike of people from other countries or cultures
Delay when nonmaterial culture struggles to adapt to changes in material culture or technology
Spread of cultural elements from one society or group to another
Passing of cultural norms and knowledge from one generation to the next
Process by which a minority group adopts the culture of the dominant group
Ideology promoting respect for and coexistence of multiple cultures within a society
First, euphoric stage of culture shock upon arriving in a new culture
Culture shock stage marked by frustration and difficulty adjusting to a new culture
Culture shock stage in which a person grows accustomed and develops a routine
Disorientation and anxiety felt when immersed in an unfamiliar culture
Lifelong process of learning the norms, values, and behaviors of one's society
Earliest socialization in childhood, largely through the family
Later socialization into smaller groups within the larger society, such as at school or work
Rehearsing behaviors in anticipation of a future social role
Discarding old behavior patterns and adopting new ones to enter a new phase of life
Person or group responsible for socializing an individual, such as family, peers, or media
Type of self-perception first developed when a child recognizes existence as a separate entity
Self-awareness that one is an 'object' that can be categorized by traits like age and gender
The way an individual perceives and evaluates himself or herself
Erikson's psychosocial crisis of adolescence about forming a stable sense of self
Case of a child raised in isolation from human contact, illustrating socialization's importance
Goffman's theatrical metaphor for how people present themselves to an 'audience'
In dramaturgy, the setting where a person performs knowing they are being observed
In dramaturgy, the private setting where a person can drop their public persona
Efforts to control the impression others form of oneself
Sociologist who developed the dramaturgical perspective and impression management
Small, intimate group with close, enduring personal relationships
Large, impersonal group organized around a specific goal or activity
Group or category an individual uses as a standard for self-evaluation
Group to which an individual feels loyalty and belonging
Group to which an individual feels no belonging or feels competition or hostility
Group of people of similar age and social status
Status that dominates all others and shapes a person's overall social identity
Social status a person is born with or assigned involuntarily
Social status a person earns through effort or accomplishment
Set of behaviors and expectations attached to a particular social status
Difficulty meeting the conflicting demands of a single role
Difficulty meeting the demands of two or more distinct roles that clash
Process of disengaging from a role that has been central to one's identity
Large, hierarchical organization governed by formal rules and impersonal positions
Weber's metaphor for entrapment by the impersonal rationality of modern bureaucracy
Theorist associated with rationalization, bureaucracy, and the Protestant work ethic
Organization people join voluntarily to pursue a shared cause or interest
Organization people join for practical, tangible reward such as pay
Organization people are forced to join, such as a prison or mandatory institution
Sociological network property describing many acquaintances providing access to information
Sociological network property describing close relationships providing emotional support
Improved performance on a task in the presence of others
Reduced individual effort when working in a group
Group discussion strengthening members' pre-existing attitudes toward an extreme
Faulty group decision making driven by desire for harmony and conformity
Theorist who coined the term groupthink
Janis's term for group members who shield the group from dissenting information
Failure of bystanders to help a victim, made likelier by the presence of others
Case whose murder popularized the study of the bystander effect
Loss of individual self-awareness and restraint within a group or crowd
Adjusting one's behavior or beliefs to match those of a group
Social influence in which peers encourage a person to change to fit group norms
Prestige and honor attached to a person's position or occupation
Preconceived, often negative, attitude or belief about a group
Unfair treatment or action directed toward an individual or group
Oversimplified, generalized belief about the members of a group
Model categorizing stereotypes by the dimensions of warmth and competence
Anxiety that one's performance will confirm a negative stereotype about one's group
Prediction that causes itself to come true by influencing behavior toward the expectation
Classroom effect in which teachers' expectations influence student performance
Discrimination built into the policies and practices of social institutions
Belief that the world is fair and people get what they deserve
Blaming an innocent individual or group for a problem they did not cause
Policy giving preferential opportunities to underrepresented or disadvantaged groups
Negative social label that devalues and discredits an individual
Loss of a group's rights, especially the right to vote
Hidden lessons about norms and values that schools teach beyond the formal curriculum
Brain structure whose activation is linked to fear and aggression in prejudice studies
Discrimination arising from criteria that appear neutral but produce unequal outcomes
Kinship system tracing descent through both the mother's and father's lines
Kinship system tracing descent through the father's line
Kinship system tracing descent through the mother's line
Family or society in which authority is held by males, especially the father
Family or society in which authority is held by females
Family social benefits include companionship, socialization, and protection but not this demanded trait
Small religious group that has broken away from an established larger religion
New religious movement with beliefs and practices outside societal norms, often led by one charismatic figure
Government in which religious leaders rule in the name of a deity
Officially established religion of a nation
Government controlled by a small group of powerful or wealthy people
Government or society specifically controlled by its wealthiest members
Economic system in which private owners control the means of production for profit
Systematic dissemination of biased information to shape public attitudes
Process by which a society becomes increasingly connected economically and culturally worldwide
Process of defining a nonmedical problem or behavior as a medical condition
Theorist who defined the rights and obligations of the 'sick role'
Sick-role right releasing the ill person from normal duties and responsibilities
Sick-role obligation to seek help and cooperate with medical professionals to get well
cards.back: Duty to try to get well

cards.front: Study of the distribution and social determinants of health and disease in populations
Shift toward large insurer-managed hospital systems described by this bureaucratic process
Ethical principle of acting for the patient's benefit
Ethical principle of avoiding harm to the patient
Ethical principle of respecting a patient's right to make their own decisions
Ethical principle of fair distribution of benefits and burdens in care
Research ethics requirement that participants agree with full knowledge of the study
Study in which black men with syphilis were left untreated, a landmark ethics violation
Woman whose cancer cells (HeLa) were used in research without her consent
Differences in health outcomes and access closely tied to social and economic disadvantage
Area with limited access to affordable, nutritious food, linked to poor diet outcomes
Disproportionate placement of environmental hazards in disadvantaged communities
Ranking of people into a hierarchy of social and economic layers
A person's combined economic and social position based on income, education, and occupation
Stratification system based on economic position that permits movement between levels
Rigid stratification system in which status is fixed at birth and mobility is impossible
Stratification system rewarding individuals on the basis of ability and effort
Stratification system that permits movement between social strata
Stratification system that prevents movement between social strata
Movement of an individual or group up or down the social hierarchy
Change in social position within a single individual's lifetime
Change in social position across generations of a family
Transmission of social and economic status from parents to children across generations
Awareness of one's position within the class structure and shared interests with that class
Marx's term for false understanding of one's true class position and interests
Poverty defined by inability to meet basic needs for survival
Poverty defined relative to the standard of living of others in one's society
Networks of relationships that provide resources and opportunities
Nonfinancial assets like education, skills, and tastes that promote social mobility
Massey and Denton's set of measures quantifying dimensions of residential segregation
Feeling of isolation and powerlessness in a segregated, resource-poor neighborhood lacking job connections
Statistical study of the size, structure, and distribution of human populations
Model describing population shifts as a society industrializes, proposed by Warren Thompson
Number of live births per woman or per 1,000 people in a population
Number of deaths per 1,000 people in a population
Ratio of males to females in a population, normally about 1:1
Movement of people into a country to settle
Movement of people out of a country to settle elsewhere
General movement of people from one place to another
Demographic transition stage with high birth and death rates and stable population
Demographic transition stage with falling death rates causing rapid population growth
Demographic transition stage in which fertility falls below mortality, shrinking the population
Factor NOT responsible for the falling death rate of demographic transition stage 2
Number of individuals of a species that an environment can sustainably support
Growth in the proportion of a population living in cities
Population movement from central cities to surrounding suburbs
Return of higher-income residents to a poorer urban area, often displacing residents
Temporary, novel, and short-lived interest or behavior adopted by many people
Rapid spread of intense, often irrational fear or anxiety through a population
Widespread reaction of fear that a group or issue threatens society's values
Crowd theory holding that people in crowds lose rationality through a hypnotic, anonymous influence
Crowd theory holding that crowds form because like-minded individuals come together
Crowd theory holding that crowds develop new norms in ambiguous situations that guide behavior
Collective, organized effort to promote or resist social change
Violation of a society's norms
Deviance involving violation of formally codified rules or laws
Deviance involving violation of unwritten social norms
Penalty imposed by law for formal deviance
Informal social penalty such as disapproval or ridicule for norm violation
Theory that deviance results from the label society attaches to an act or person
Merton's theory that deviance arises from a gap between cultural goals and legitimate means
Durkheim's term for normlessness and social instability, often felt when one cannot integrate into society
Theory that deviance is learned through interaction with others who deviate
Any means, formal or informal, a society uses to enforce conformity to norms
Adding a desirable stimulus to increase a behavior
Adding an undesirable stimulus to decrease a behavior
Learning behavior by observing and imitating others
Range of characteristics from masculine to feminine that are socially constructed
A person's biological classification as male or female
A person's internal sense of their own gender
Imbalance arising from natural factors, sex-selective abortion, or differential aging
Study following the same subjects over an extended period of time
Study observing a population at a single point in time
Study group followed over time, defined by a shared characteristic
In-depth investigation of a single individual or small group
Study that looks backward using existing records to link past exposures to outcomes
Bias in which respondents answer in ways they think are socially acceptable
Extent to which study results generalize to real-world settings and populations
Form of external validity concerning how well findings apply to real-life situations
Variable that changes the strength or direction of a relationship between two variables
Uncontrolled variable that distorts the apparent relationship between studied variables
